54: <header>5. Conclusion </header>

Co-digestion with manure (or eventually seaweed) for biogas and nutrient production has appeared to be the best management option for orange peel waste, in terms of global warming and resource depletion mitigation effects. Among the alternatives considered for the final uti- lization of the obtained biogas, upgrading to biomethane for use in the transport sector appeared to be the best alternative. A drawback of all the anaerobic digestion strategies was the eutrophication impact due to nitrogen leaching as a result of the on-land application of the residual organic digestate. A preliminary cost analysis also highlighted
anaerobic mono- and co-digestion scenarios as being economically

preferable to the alternatives. Among these, animal feeding was also an environmentally appealing option, in particular because of its use as a|Doni, S., Macci, C., Longo, V., Souid, A., Garcia, C., Masciandaro, G., 2017. 55: <header>5. Conclusion </header>

Co-digestion with manure (or eventually seaweed) for biogas and nutrient production has appeared to be the best management option for orange peel waste, in terms of global warming and resource depletion mitigation effects. Among the alternatives considered for the final uti- lization of the obtained biogas, upgrading to biomethane for use in the transport sector appeared to be the best alternative. A drawback of all the anaerobic digestion strategies was the eutrophication impact due to nitrogen leaching as a result of the on-land application of the residual organic digestate. A preliminary cost analysis also highlighted
anaerobic mono- and co-digestion scenarios as being economically

preferable to the alternatives. Among these, animal feeding was also an environmentally appealing option, in particular because of its use as a|Doni, S., Macci, C., Longo, V., Souid, A., Garcia, C., Masciandaro, G., 2017. 56: <header>5. Conclusion </header>

Co-digestion with manure (or eventually seaweed) for biogas and nutrient production has appeared to be the best management option for orange peel waste, in terms of global warming and resource depletion mitigation effects. Among the alternatives considered for the final uti- lization of the obtained biogas, upgrading to biomethane for use in the transport sector appeared to be the best alternative. A drawback of all the anaerobic digestion strategies was the eutrophication impact due to nitrogen leaching as a result of the on-land application of the residual organic digestate. A preliminary cost analysis also highlighted
anaerobic mono- and co-digestion scenarios as being economically

preferable to the alternatives. 57: <header>5. Conclusion </header>

Co-digestion with manure (or eventually seaweed) for biogas and nutrient production has appeared to be the best management option for orange peel waste, in terms of global warming and resource depletion mitigation effects. Among the alternatives considered for the final uti- lization of the obtained biogas, upgrading to biomethane for use in the transport sector appeared to be the best alternative. A drawback of all the anaerobic digestion strategies was the eutrophication impact due to nitrogen leaching as a result of the on-land application of the residual organic digestate. A preliminary cost analysis also highlighted
anaerobic mono- and co-digestion scenarios as being economically

preferable to the alternatives. 58: <header>5. Conclusion </header>

Co-digestion with manure (or eventually seaweed) for biogas and nutrient production has appeared to be the best management option for orange peel waste, in terms of global warming and resource depletion mitigation effects. Among the alternatives considered for the final uti- lization of the obtained biogas, upgrading to biomethane for use in the transport sector appeared to be the best alternative. A drawback of all the anaerobic digestion strategies was the eutrophication impact due to nitrogen leaching as a result of the on-land application of the residual organic digestate. A preliminary cost analysis also highlighted
anaerobic mono- and co-digestion scenarios as being economically

preferable to the alternatives. 59: <header>5. Conclusion </header>

Co-digestion with manure (or eventually seaweed) for biogas and nutrient production has appeared to be the best management option for orange peel waste, in terms of global warming and resource depletion mitigation effects. Among the alternatives considered for the final uti- lization of the obtained biogas, upgrading to biomethane for use in the transport sector appeared to be the best alternative. A drawback of all the anaerobic digestion strategies was the eutrophication impact due to nitrogen leaching as a result of the on-land application of the residual organic digestate. A preliminary cost analysis also highlighted
anaerobic mono- and co-digestion scenarios as being economically

preferable to the alternatives.
